topic: An artist discovers someone has tattooed their work
raw_output:
[story 1]

The gallery air felt thick and suffocating, clinging to my skin like cheap varnish. I stood before the canvas, a riot of ochre and bruised violet, and then I saw it. A stark, black line, impossibly precise, winding around the central figure’s eye. It wasn't part of the composition; it was an addition, a deliberate, ugly mark. My jaw tightened. I slowly turned, my gaze sweeping over the room, landing on the artist standing near the exit. Their posture was too relaxed, a smug curve to their lips that made my stomach clench. "What is that?" My voice was low, edged with something sharp. The artist merely smiled, a slow, knowing movement that didn't reach their eyes. "A little personalization," they murmured, their tone dripping with false sincerity. I felt a sudden, hot flush rise up my neck, a desire to physically recoil from the audacity of it all.

[story 2]

I traced the edge of the print with a fingertip, my expression hardening into something cold and brittle. The texture of the paint felt offensively vibrant under my touch. Then, my eyes snagged on the subject's wrist. Beneath the swirling depiction of celestial bodies, a tattoo—a miniature, intricate portrait of the artist themselves. A slow, deliberate breath escaped me, tasting like ash. I straightened up, the movement stiff, every muscle in my body coiled tight. "You took liberties," I stated, the words clipped and devoid of warmth. I watched the artist’s reaction—a flicker of surprise, quickly masked by practiced nonchalance. I didn't wait for an explanation. I simply turned my back on the piece, the movement sharp and final, walking toward the door as if the very air around that person was contaminated.

[story 3]

It was a small detail, almost overlooked, but it snagged my attention like a splinter. The brushstrokes were masterful, undeniably so, but there, nestled in the shadow of a drapery fold, was a symbol—a stylized raven, inked with a disturbing realism. I leaned in, my breath catching in my throat, and then I saw it: the raven’s wing seemed to flow directly into the pattern of the artist’s own signature, a deliberate, parasitic connection. A slow, unpleasant smile stretched across my face. I straightened up, pushing my hands deep into my pockets, the gesture radiating a distinct lack of interest. "That," I said, my voice flat, "is rather presumptuous." I didn't offer any further comment, just a steady, unwavering stare that seemed to measure the depth of their audacity.
